article_title: ESA Postpones SMILE Launch After Vega-C Component Issue Surfaces Days Before Liftoff

content_preview.title: ESA Postpones SMILE Launch After Vega-C Component Issue Surfaces Days Before Liftoff
content_preview.summary: A subsystem production defect forced Avio to delay the Vega-C VV29 flight carrying ESA and China's SMILE magnetosphere-imaging mission, originally set for April 9, with no new date confirmed.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The European Space Agency's joint mission with the Chinese Academy of Sciences to image Earth's magnetosphere in X-rays has been postponed just days before its scheduled liftoff. Avio, the Italian launch provider responsible for the Vega-C rocket, announced on April 5 that a technical issue discovered on a subsystem component production line after launcher integration required further investigation, according to [ESA](https://www.esa.int/Science_Exploration/Space_Science/Smile/Smile...

editor_notes.content_quality: Comprehensive space article covering both the delay and full mission context. Good cross-reference to prior SMILE coverage.
editor_notes.source_verification: 3 sources checked. ESA launch page: VERIFIED — confirms April 5 postponement, subsystem component production line issue, VV29, Avio investigation. ESA factsheet: accessible, mission specs (instruments, orbit, mass) sourced from official ESA documentation. Space.com: VERIFIED — confirms ESA-CAS joint mission structure, payload/platform division, Antarctic and China ground stations.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All technical specs (2300kg, 4 instruments, 121000km apogee, 5000km perigee, Apr 8-May 7 window) sourced from ESA factsheet. Vega-C grounding history (Dec 2022 failure, Dec 2024 return) is well-documented public knowledge.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral. Covers delay without speculation, includes both operational impact and scientific significance.
editor_notes.originality: Follow-up to prior SMILE arrival coverage (2026-03/19). Properly cross-referenced.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Strong space mission article with proper continuity. All sources verified. Approved.
